items.title: SA wildlife carers 'ground zero' for third H5 bird flu case

children.content: A South Australian wildlife rescue group is working with local authorities after Australia's third confirmed case of H5 bird flu was detected in a migratory bird.

articles.title: Deadly bird flu discovered in second Australian state

children.content: A third confirmed case of the deadly H5 bird flu has been found in a migratory bird on Australia's southern coast.

image.altText: Warnings a much deadlier strain of avian influenza could arrive this spring

articles.title: Australia's mammals are at risk of bird flu. This is what we know

children.content: While birds are the most likely victims of the deadly strain of bird flu, researchers are also sounding the alarm for native mammals and marsupial species. These are some of the steps being taken to protect them.

image.altText: small black animal with white stripe across chest looking at straight at the viewer, standing over dead animal

thumbnail.altText: A giant petrel bird hides in green seaside foliage at Knights Beach on the Fleurieu Peninsula.

items.title: How effective are vaccines at stopping deadly bird flu from spreading?

children.content: With bird flu arriving on our shores, governments, wildlife groups and agricultural organisations are springing into action. But can vaccines limit the economic and environmental damage of the deadly influenza?

image.altText: A veterinarian injects a vaccine into a chick in an industry chicken farm

items.title: Animal cruelty charges laid a year on from piggery footage

children.content: A year after footage from the site was widely circulated the RSPCA has pressed animal cruelty charges against people connected to Andgar Piggery.

items.title: Understanding of harmful algal blooms still murky, disaster survey finds

children.content: Most Australians remain uncertain about the cause of harmful algal blooms and are more worried about disasters such as bushfires and flooding.

items.title: Murray-Darling Basin communities call for balance in review

children.content: Communities across the Murray-Darling Basin are calling for a better balance, as irrigators say water buybacks are hurting their communities.

image.altText: A view of the River Murray from a cliff, flanked by trees. The sky is blue.

items.title: 'Significant errors' in 'preventable' death of baby in murder-suicide

children.content: A coroner finds the "upsetting" death of a nine-month-old baby in the Barossa Valley in a public murder-suicide by her "manipulative, self-absorbed and dangerous" father could have been prevented.

items.title: Kangaroo Island declared feral pig-free after more than two centuries

children.content: Devastating bushfires provide Kangaroo Island with a unique silver lining as it becomes the largest island in the world to eradicate feral pigs.

image.altText: A dark coloured feral pig in bushland

items.title: SA greyhound trainer banned for dog's 'unnecessary suffering'

children.content: One of SA greyhound racing's most prominent identities has been disqualified after a 12-month inquiry by the sport's controlling body.

items.title: Nose landing gear failure could have caused fatal plane crash, ATSB says

children.content: A plane crash that killed two people and injured several others in Adelaide earlier this year could have been caused by a failure in the plane's nose landing gear, an ongoing investigation has found.

items.title: Woman fights to have ex-policeman husband kept behind bars

items.title: SA pushes for AI data centres as industry says it's not asking for subsidies

children.content: The South Australian government is pushing for the state to become home to billions of dollars' worth of artificial intelligence data centres, but says it will seek to regulate their water and energy use.

items.title: Adelaide man 'not criminally responsible' for housemate's murder, court finds

children.content: An Adelaide court has found Kristopher Robert Steele fatally stabbed his housemate, but is "not criminally responsible due to mental incompetence".

items.title: As Ebola spreads, Australia faces rise in 'biblical' disease risks

children.content: The bugs that cause Ebola, hantavirus and diphtheria are microscopic, but many of the barriers to controlling them are human-sized.

items.title: Olympian arrested at reflecting pool is Trump 'scapegoat', lawyer says

children.content: A lawyer for the Olympian arrested at Washington's reflecting pool says his client is a scapegoat who was targeted to distract Americans from the Trump administration's failures.

articles.title: Who is going to qualify for the World Cup knockout rounds?

children.content: The Socceroos will finish either second of third in their World Cup group. Second means qualification for the knockout rounds. Third is a bit more complicated.

items.title: Australians' tricks for dealing with heatwaves that Europeans yet to learn

children.content: Europe's record-breaking heatwave has highlighted its vulnerability to climate change. But countries could look to Australia's experience with scorching summers to learn valuable lessons.

items.title: House prices in two Australian capitals tipped to fall substantially

children.content: House price falls of up to 7 and 8 per cent in Sydney and Melbourne have been forecast for the year ahead, according to Domain. But it is far from doom and gloom for existing owners, with resale profits hitting a record.

items.title: The alternative burial method that could soon come to Australia

children.content: Advocates say human composting offers a sustainable, greener alternative to burials and crematoriums, and can also provide a cheaper and equitable option after death.

items.title: Former Liberal vice-president Teena McQueen dies after short illness

items.title: Small designers hitting roadblocks trying to get support against Shein copies

items.title: Family of 17yo killed in dog attack want mandatory owner licences

items.title: Remote community's decade-long wait for clean drinking water

children.content: Residents of Pandanus Park continue their near-daily 600-metre walk to access safe drinking water in a decade-long wait for a solution to dangerous nitrate in their tap water.
